title: Making biochar in a retort burn barrel
subtitle: A simple top-lit updraft or retort system for small-scale biochar production in the garden
excerpt: Biochar is charcoal made from plant material (wood, straw, prunings) through pyrolysis: heating in limited oxygen so the material chars without combusting fully. The result is a highly porous carbon that, incorporated into soil at 1-5 kg/m2, improves water and nutrient retention. Making it in a garden barrel retort costs almost nothing if you have a source of woody prunings.
sourceNotes: International Biochar Initiative technical specifications for agricultural biochar. RHS guidance on biochar soil amendment. CAT (Centre for Alternative Technology) biochar production guide. TLUD (Top-Lit Updraft) gasifier design principles. Biochar Research Centre at Edinburgh University small-scale production documentation.

projectSchedule.title: Quench and cure
projectSchedule.body: After the burn barrel cools overnight, add the quenched biochar to a bucket of water with a handful of compost to begin charging. Leave for 2-4 weeks before application.

content.text: Biochar is made by heating woody material to 300-700°C with restricted oxygen, so it chars rather than burns to ash. The result is a porous carbon with a surface area of 100-300 m2 per gram. It holds water, plant nutrients (potassium, calcium, magnesium), and soil microorganisms. A simple retort barrel using garden prunings and scrap metal produces small batches of usable biochar in about 3 hours.

Before you start. Work outdoors in a well-ventilated area, away from structures. The barrel and contents will be extremely hot during and after the burn. Keep a bucket of water and a fire extinguisher within reach. Check local rules on burning in the garden (smoke control area regulations apply to open burning, not enclosed retort systems, but check your council's guidance).
An inner barrel (20-50 litre metal drum) with a lid and a small air inlet hole drilled at the base (25 mm diameter)
An outer barrel (larger drum) open at the top, which creates the firing chamber around the inner
Dry hardwood or fruit-tree prunings cut to 200-300 mm lengths, moisture content below 20% (dried for at least 6 months outdoors)
Fill the inner barrel loosely with dry woody material. Leave 50 mm headspace.
Seal the inner barrel lid (it need not be airtight, just resting in place with the small inlet hole clear).
Place the inner barrel in the outer barrel. Build a small fire of scrap wood in the outer barrel around the inner one.

Keep the outer fire burning for 2-3 hours. The inner barrel heats to pyrolysis temperature. You will see thin white smoke from the inner lid vent changing to thicker blue-grey pyrolysis gas smoke, then clearing as the process completes.

When the smoke clears and the outer fire dies down naturally, the pyrolysis is complete. Seal the inner barrel inlet with a metal plate or soil to exclude oxygen, and leave to cool for at least 8 hours.
Quench the cooled biochar by pouring water slowly into the barrel. Do not rush: steam is released. Stir with a stick to ensure full wetting.

Fresh biochar is porous but contains almost no nutrients. Applied straight to soil, it may initially lock up nitrogen by adsorbing it from the soil before microbial communities colonise it. 'Charge' the biochar before use: soak it in compost leachate, diluted urine (1:10), or compost tea for 2-4 weeks. The pores fill with nutrients and microorganisms, making the material immediately beneficial rather than temporarily depleting.
